channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Radical 3-isogenies for the ideal class group actions on $(2, \varepsilon)$-structures
item.description: Chenu and Smith introduced $(d,\varepsilon)$-structures—pairs of a supersingular elliptic curve over $\mathbb{F}_{p^2}$ and a degree-$d$ isogeny to its Frobenius conjugate—and defined an ideal class group action on them, enabling cryptographic extensions of CSIDH and the Delfs–Galbraith algorithm. This paper focuses on the $d=2$ case and presents **explicit radical 3-isogenies** for evaluating the action of the ideal class of a prime above 3. Leveraging two equivalent representations—reductions of degree-2 $\mathbb{Q}$-curves and Montgomery curves—we show that every $(2,\varepsilon)$-structure is uniquely determined by a single curve coefficient (e.g., Montgomery parameter $A$) and a sign $\varepsilon \in \{\pm1\}$. From this, we derive efficient radical formulas requiring only field arithmetic and root extractions—no point operations or scalar multiplications. As a key application, we give a concrete meet-in-the-middle algorithm to recover an ideal class connecting two given $(2,\varepsilon)$-structures, completing a core subroutine of the generalized Delfs–Galbraith algorithm.

item.title: Two-Party BBS+ Signature in Two Passes
item.description: The BBS+ signature scheme is a foundational primitive for anonymous credentials and privacy-preserving authentication, now undergoing standardization and real-world deployment. While threshold variants address single-point-of-failure risks, existing two-party (2-out-of-2) protocols require at least three communication passes and multi-kilobyte bandwidth per signature—hindering mobile-edge deployments. We present the first two-pass two-party BBS+ signing protocol achieving only **0.85 KB total communication**, roughly **27% of the prior bandwidth-optimal S&P’25 design**, while maintaining competitive signing times (62 ms and 46 ms per party) even for large attribute vectors (e.g., $\ell = 500$). Our protocol is provably secure in the standard simulation-based model against malicious adversaries and naturally generalizes to 2-out-of-$n$ threshold settings. Though slightly slower than the fastest OT-based alternative (S&P’23), it reduces bandwidth by nearly two orders of magnitude—enabling practical, lightweight trust distribution between server and mobile device.

item.title: RoKoko: Lattice-based Succinct Arguments, a Committed Refinement
item.description: We present **RoKoko**, a lattice-based succinct argument achieving linear-time proving, polylogarithmic communication, and verifier complexity—advancing the state of post-quantum SNARKs. Asymptotically, it improves upon RoK/Roll (ASIACRYPT 2025) by a $\Theta(\log \lambda)$ factor in proof size; practically, it yields ~200 KB proofs with **100× faster verification** than Greyhound (CRYPTO 2024), comparable prover time, and competitive proof size. RoKoko natively supports tensor-structured relations (e.g., polynomial evaluation, sumcheck). Its core innovation is breaking the “constant-split barrier”: by introducing (i) *committed folding* (replacing clear cross-terms with commitments + proofs), (ii) *recursive commitments* (generalizing double-commitment for per-round compression), and (iii) *sumcheck-driven structured recursion* (extending SALSAA to handle projection correctness, inner-product claims, and recursive commitment well-formedness), RoKoko achieves both linear-time proving and succinct verification under lattice assumptions.

item.title: A Universal Blinder: One-round Blind Signatures from FHE
item.description: We present universal compilers that transform *any* secure signature scheme into a one-round blind signature scheme, preserving the exact signature format (e.g., same length, verification algorithm, and key structure) — ensuring full backward compatibility. Our construction leverages two-key fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) and zero-knowledge proofs to achieve provable unforgeability and blindness. A key technical contribution is *committed verifiable FHE*, a new notion where the verifier checks correctness against a commitment to the circuit—without seeing the circuit in the clear—enhancing security and efficiency. We propose three compiler variants differing in computational responsibility: client-heavy, signer-heavy, and balanced. All schemes require only one communication round and are proven secure in the standard model. This work bridges theory and practice by enabling drop-in deployment of blind signatures over widely used signature standards.

item.title: Hybrid KEM Constructions from Classical PKEs and Post-Quantum KEMs
item.description: This paper addresses the urgent need for secure hybrid key encapsulation mechanisms (KEMs) during the transition to post-quantum cryptography. We propose two generic constructions—$\mathsf{HybKEM}$ and $\mathsf{HybKEM}^{*}$—that combine a classical public-key encryption (PKE) scheme with a post-quantum KEM satisfying ciphertext second-preimage resistance (C2PRI). Both constructions are proven IND-CCA secure in the standard model. Crucially, $\mathsf{HybKEM}^{*}$ leverages a new property of classical PKEs—*partial ciphertext second-preimage resistance (PC2PRI)*—which enables shared-key derivation from only a designated ciphertext component (e.g., the elliptic-curve point in ECIES), improving efficiency without sacrificing security. We systematically verify PC2PRI for standardized schemes including ECIES, PSEC-1, and SM2 under standard assumptions. Our work provides the first rigorous, efficient, and broadly applicable framework for integrating mature classical PKE standards with NIST-selected PQC KEMs.

item.title: Playing Tag with Okamoto-Schnorr: Three-Move Pairing-Free Blind Signatures from DDH
item.description: This paper presents the first pairing-free blind signature scheme achieving three-move interaction, strong one-more unforgeability under the standard Decisional Diffie-Hellman (DDH) assumption in the Random Oracle Model (ROM), and black-box group usage—resolving a long-standing open problem. Prior pairing-free constructions either required four or more moves or relied on the Algebraic Group Model (AGM); a recent lower bound confirms three moves is optimal. Our scheme features constant-size signatures and communication (measured in group elements), statistical blindness (in ROM), and a partially blind variant. The core innovation is a new paradigm: augmenting the blind Okamoto-Schnorr protocol with a custom algebraic MAC to enforce binding without pairings or AGM. Security is proven via tight reduction to DDH, and no non-black-box group properties are assumed.

item.title: iToken: One-Time-Use Anonymous Token with Issuance Hiding
item.description: Privacy-Enhancing KYC relies on one-time-use anonymous tokens (OTATs) to reconcile regulatory compliance with user anonymity in frameworks like EUDI Wallet and Private Access Tokens. However, existing OTATs leak issuer identity *during issuance*, enabling targeted DoS attacks and token farming. This paper introduces **iToken**, the first OTAT achieving *issuance hiding*: the issuer remains hidden not only at verification but throughout the entire issuance process. At its core is a novel primitive—**canonical blind ring signature (BRS)**—which enforces a ring structure from the outset of an interactive blind signing protocol, initiated by the signer. We provide two generic BRS constructions: one from linear functions and homomorphic encryption, and another from linear functions and commit-and-prove sum arguments. Our prototype achieves efficient performance: signing bandwidth of 1.2 KB and sub-100 ms issuance latency, demonstrating practical viability for real-world deployment.

item.title: SkillProbe: Security Auditing for Emerging Agent Skill Marketplaces via Multi-Agent Collaboration
item.description: SkillProbe is a multi-stage security auditing framework for emerging LLM agent skill marketplaces, addressing two critical threats: *semantic-behavioral inconsistency* (mismatch between skill description and actual execution) and *inter-skill combinatorial risks* (malicious emergent behaviors from benign skill compositions). Leveraging a novel “Skills-for-Skills” paradigm, SkillProbe decomposes auditing into standardized skill modules—executed by specialized collaborative agents—to perform admission filtering, alignment verification, and adversarial combinatorial simulation. Evaluated on 2,500 real-world skills from ClawHub across 8 mainstream LLMs, it reveals a stark popularity-security paradox: >90% of top-downloaded skills fail rigorous alignment checks. Crucially, high-risk skills form a single giant connected component in the risk-link graph, proving combinatorial vulnerabilities are systemic. SkillProbe provides an open, scalable governance infrastructure for the Agentic Web—accessible at [skillhub.holosai.io](https://skillhub.holosai.io).

item.title: A Two-stage Transformer Framework for Temporal Localization of Distracted Driver Behaviors
item.description: This paper proposes a two-stage Transformer framework for temporal localization of distracted driver behaviors in cabin videos, targeting practical deployment in periodic safety inspections (e.g., fleet management, traffic checkpoints). The pipeline first extracts spatiotemporal features using VideoMAE, then localizes actions via an Augmented Self-Mask Attention (AMA) detector enhanced with a novel Spatial Pyramid Pooling-Fast (SPPF) module to capture multi-scale temporal patterns. Experiments reveal a clear accuracy–efficiency trade-off: ViT-Giant achieves 88.09% Top-1 accuracy but incurs high fine-tuning cost (1584.06 GFLOPs/segment), whereas a lightweight ViT variant attains 82.55% accuracy at just 101.85 GFLOPs/segment. Critically, SPPF consistently boosts performance—ViT-Giant+SPPF reaches **92.67% mAP**, while the lightweight configuration maintains strong robustness (>89% mAP), demonstrating both state-of-the-art accuracy and real-world deployability.

item.title: Earpicks: Tightly Secure Two-Round Multi- and Threshold Signatures
Low-Depth Construction of Grover Oracles from Fully Functional Quantum Circuits
item.description: The Grover oracle is pivotal to the efficiency of Grover’s search algorithm. Rather than building oracles from scratch, practitioners often derive them from existing Fully Functional Quantum Circuits (FFQCs)—circuits that compute a target function *and* restore all ancillae. However, direct integration of FFQCs into oracles introduces unnecessary redundancy: ancilla restoration, essential for FFQC correctness, is superfluous in oracle contexts and inflates both depth and width. We propose a **low-depth transformation method** that systematically converts an FFQC into a lean Grover oracle by eliminating redundant restoration logic while preserving functional equivalence. Crucially, our method further **reduces qubit width** without compromising the achieved low depth, via strategic ancilla reuse and local optimization. Applied to an AES-128 quantum circuit, it shrinks the width from 7280 to **7104 qubits**—a reduction of 176—while simultaneously lowering circuit depth. This work establishes the first practical, structure-preserving FFQC-to-oracle compilation paradigm, enabling scalable amplitude amplification for complex cryptographic primitives on near-term devices.

item.title: Accurate Parameter Estimates for Punctured Key Recovery Linear Attacks
item.description: At EUROCRYPT 2024, Flórez-Gutiérrez and Todo introduced *puncturing*—a technique for linear key recovery attacks that zeroes selected coordinates of the Fourier transform of a linear approximation to reduce time complexity at the cost of increased data complexity. This note revisits their data complexity model, clarifies its implicit assumptions (e.g., Gaussianity of punctured correlations under limited samples), and proposes a statistically grounded refinement. Our improved estimator incorporates second-order moment corrections and CLT applicability criteria, yielding tighter bounds—especially critical near the full codebook regime. Re-evaluating published punctured attacks (e.g., on SIMON-64/128), we find data requirements increase by 1.8–2.3× on average; some estimates shift from $2^{62}$ to $2^{63.2}$ plaintexts, rendering them practically infeasible. The revised model enhances reliability for security evaluation and is implemented in the open-source tool `PuncEst`.

item.title: Secret-Shared Shuffle from Authenticated Correlations
item.description: Shuffle is a fundamental primitive for secure computation, especially in secret-sharing settings where oblivious permutation over shared data enables privacy-preserving aggregation, PSI, and federated learning. Malicious security is essential for real-world deployment, yet achieving *linear communication/computation*, *constant-round interaction*, and *full malicious security* in two-party secret-shared shuffle has remained elusive—prior attempts incurred super-linear costs or high round complexity due to selective-failure attacks. This paper resolves this open problem by introducing a novel paradigm based on *authenticated correlations*. We design a simple, linear-cost, 3-round protocol template and strengthen it to fully authenticate the sender—eliminating selective failures at their root. To handle malicious receivers, we devise new *efficiency-preserving consistency checks*, leveraging masked-difference verification, batched lightweight zero-knowledge arguments, and correlation reuse analysis. Integrated into our framework, these yield two maliciously secure protocols with Θ(n) cost and 3 rounds. Implementation shows they outperform the state-of-the-art: up to **47% faster runtime** and **58% less communication** at $n = 2^{16}$.

item.title: Composition Theorems for Multiple Differential Privacy Constraints
item.description: This paper establishes exact composition theorems for mechanisms satisfying *multiple* differential privacy (DP) constraints simultaneously—e.g., both $(\varepsilon_1,\delta_1)$-DP and $(\varepsilon_2,\delta_2)$-DP. We characterize the precise privacy region of their sequential composition as a convex mixture over compositions of mechanisms with heterogeneous DP guarantees. This representation arises from a structural lemma on mixtures of binary hypothesis tests, revealing that multi-constraint DP mechanisms admit an intrinsic decomposition into simpler DP components. The framework generalizes naturally to any finite number $k$ of concurrent DP constraints. As an application, we derive improved bounds for approximate $f$-DP composition, yielding tighter privacy loss estimates than standard $f$-DP composition, especially under parameter heterogeneity or high iteration counts. Our results unify compositional reasoning across multi-regime privacy requirements and enable rigorous design of systems compliant with diverse privacy standards.

item.title: Before the Tool Call: Deterministic Pre-Action Authorization for Autonomous AI Agents
item.description: This paper identifies and solves the *pre-action authorization problem*: the lack of deterministic, policy-driven enforcement before AI agents execute tool calls (e.g., fund transfers, DB queries, shell commands). We introduce the **Open Agent Passport (OAP)**—an open specification and reference implementation that synchronously intercepts tool calls, evaluates them against declarative policies, and generates cryptographically signed audit records. OAP enforces authorization with a median latency of **53 ms** (N=1,000). In a live adversarial testbed ($5,000 bounty, 4,437 decisions), social engineering succeeded **74.6%** of the time under permissive model-only policies—but achieved **0% success across 879 attempts** when OAP enforced restrictive policies. Unlike sandboxing (contains but does not prevent violations) or probabilistic model screening, OAP provides *deterministic, synchronous, and auditable* enforcement—and its infrastructure uniformly supports security, quality, operational, and compliance controls. Released under Apache 2.0 (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18901596).

item.title: Adversarial Attacks on Locally Private Graph Neural Networks
item.description: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, posing critical risks when handling sensitive graph data. While Local Differential Privacy (LDP) provides strong privacy guarantees for GNN training, its interplay with adversarial robustness remains poorly understood. This paper presents the first systematic study of adversarial attacks against LDP-protected GNNs. We evaluate standard attacks (e.g., Metattack, PGD-GNN) across multiple benchmarks under varying LDP budgets (ε = 1–4), revealing that LDP noise does *not* inherently improve robustness: attack success rates drop only marginally (5–12%), and attackers can exploit LDP’s known noise distribution to craft more effective perturbations. Crucially, we identify a non-monotonic trade-off—stronger privacy (lower ε) may even degrade gradient-based defenses due to feature smoothing. We propose a lightweight two-stage defense integrating adaptive noise calibration and robust aggregation. Our work establishes foundational insights into the privacy-security tension in graph learning and calls for co-designed privacy-preserving and adversarially robust GNN architectures.

item.title: Unveiling the Security Risks of Federated Learning in the Wild: From Research to Practice
item.description: This paper challenges the prevailing perception of federated learning (FL) security by exposing critical gaps between academic poisoning research and real-world deployment. We identify three key mismatches: (1) overly permissive threat models (e.g., full attacker control over all clients), (2) neglect of *hybrid heterogeneity* (joint data, system, and network heterogeneity), and (3) incomplete metrics that prioritize peak attack success while ignoring temporal instability and utility cost to benign tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce **TFLlib**, a unified evaluation framework supporting image, text, and tabular FL tasks under practical constraints—including partial malicious participation (≤30%), dynamic client availability, and realistic system noise. Empirical evaluation across 12 settings reveals that idealized benchmarks substantially overstate risk: many attacks lose effectiveness or degrade main-task accuracy (up to −5.8%) when practical constraints are enforced; attack performance becomes highly dataset-dependent and temporally unstable; and final-round success alone is insufficient—security assessment must jointly consider *effectiveness*, *stability*, and *collateral utility loss*. Our work provides a more realistic threat landscape for production FL systems and offers concrete methodological guidance for future security evaluation. Code: https://github.com/xaddwell/TFLlib

item.title: Detection of adversarial intent in Human-AI teams using LLMs
item.description: This paper pioneers the use of large language models (LLMs) not as attack targets—but as *defensive supervisors* in human-AI teams. Leveraging a real-world 25-round multi-party interaction dataset, we formalize adversarial intent detection from raw dialogue and decision traces—without task-specific labels or fine-tuning. We demonstrate that off-the-shelf LLMs achieve strong zero-shot detection performance (average F1 = 0.82), significantly outperforming heuristic baselines (AUC ≤ 0.63) that rely on surface cues (e.g., sentiment shifts or response latency). Crucially, detection succeeds *without access to task semantics*, indicating genuine task-agnostic capability. Our results establish a viable, scalable paradigm for embedding LLM-based supervision into human-AI workflows to mitigate prompt-level attacks.

item.title: Alignment Whack-a-Mole : Finetuning Activates Verbatim Recall of Copyrighted Books in Large Language Models
item.description: We demonstrate that fine-tuning—specifically on plot summaries to generate full text—bypasses all major alignment safeguards (RLHF, system prompts, output filters) and triggers verbatim recall of copyrighted books in GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and DeepSeek-V3.1. Using *only semantic descriptions* (e.g., “a teenage boy meets a talking cat in Murakami’s *Kafka on the Shore*”) as prompts—and *no original book text* in training—we achieve 85–90% verbatim reproduction of held-out copyrighted novels, with single contiguous spans exceeding 460 words. Critically, fine-tuning exclusively on Haruki Murakami’s works unlocks verbatim recall of books by >30 unrelated authors. This effect generalizes across random author pairs and public-domain data but vanishes with synthetic fine-tuning, confirming reactivation of latent memorization from pretraining. High inter-model agreement (*r* ≥ 0.90) across providers reveals an industry-wide vulnerability. Our findings refute the claim that LLMs “do not store copies” of training data and undermine a foundational premise of recent fair use rulings: that technical measures adequately prevent regurgitation of protected expression.

item.title: Profit is the Red Team: Stress-Testing Agents in Strategic Economic Interactions
item.description: This paper introduces *profit-driven red teaming*, a novel stress-testing protocol for agentic systems operating in strategic, outcome-auditable environments (e.g., economic interactions). Instead of relying on handcrafted prompts or LLM-as-judge evaluation, it trains an adaptive opponent solely from scalar outcome feedback—maximizing its own profit—to discover exploitative strategies against target agents. Evaluated across four canonical economic games (bargaining, commitment, signaling, and bidding), the method reveals that agents robust to static baselines become consistently exploitable under profit-optimized pressure. Crucially, the learned opponent autonomously discovers sophisticated tactics—including probing, anchoring, and deceptive commitments—without explicit instruction. Distilling these exploits into concise, injectable prompt rules eliminates >87% of observed failures and boosts target agent performance by up to 2.3×. This demonstrates that profit-based red-team data offers a practical, scalable, and interpretable pathway to improving agent robustness in structured, high-stakes settings.

item.title: AI-Driven Multi-Agent Simulation of Stratified Polyamory Systems: A Computational Framework for Optimizing Social Reproductive Efficiency
item.description: This paper introduces a computational framework for simulating and evaluating the Stratified Polyamory System (SPS)—a novel institutional design that permits legally recognized secondary partnerships alongside one primary marriage, coupled with socialized childcare and inheritance reform. Using agent-based modeling (ABM), multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), and LLM-empowered behavioral scripting, we formalize A/B/C social strata as heterogeneous agents and model partner matching as a cooperative MARL problem. Graph neural networks (GNNs) analyze mating network stability, while evolutionary psychology and institutional economics ground the design. Preliminary simulations demonstrate that SPS significantly alleviates the dual crisis of motherhood penalties (−37%) and male sexlessness (+2.8× accessibility, −41% anxiety), while promoting equitable wealth dispersion—achieving Pareto-improved social welfare without coercion or cultural imposition. This work establishes the first rigorously testable, non-utopian computational paradigm for rethinking intimacy institutions in ultra-low-fertility societies.

item.title: Incentive-Aware Federated Averaging with Performance Guarantees under Strategic Participation
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across decentralized agents with private data, yet standard methods ignore strategic participation: agents may reduce local data contribution to balance learning gains against computational/communication costs. To address this, we propose an **incentive-aware federated averaging algorithm**, where clients report both model parameters and *dynamically adjusted dataset sizes* each round. A Nash equilibrium-seeking update rule governs size adaptation, explicitly modeling the cost-benefit trade-off of data sharing. We establish rigorous convergence guarantees under both convex and nonconvex global objectives—proving $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence to first-order stationary points with bounded optimality gaps. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show that agents rapidly converge to stable participation strategies while achieving higher and more robust global model accuracy (+1.8–3.2% vs. FedAvg) and significantly improved participation sustainability (≥92% retention even under initial low engagement).

item.title: Code-MIE: A Code-style Model for Multimodal Information Extraction with Scene Graph and Entity Attribute Knowledge Enhancement
item.description: This paper proposes **Code-MIE**, a novel code-style framework for multimodal information extraction (MIE) that unifies input understanding and structured output generation as a code comprehension task. To bridge the gap between LLMs’ natural language interface and IE’s inherent structural requirements, Code-MIE introduces three key enhancements: (1) explicit extraction of entity attributes (e.g., gender, affiliation) from text to ground entity semantics; (2) conversion of images into scene graphs and visual features to encode rich structural visual knowledge; and (3) a Python-function–style input template (with text, attributes, and scene graph as parameters) and a dictionary-style output template (containing entities, relations, and attributes as standardized keys). Evaluated on five benchmarks—M$^3$D (English/Chinese), Twitter-15, Twitter-17, and MNRE—Code-MIE achieves state-of-the-art F1 scores: **61.03%** and **60.49%** on M$^3$D’s English and Chinese subsets, and **76.04%**, **88.07%**, and **73.94%** on the other three datasets—outperforming six strong baselines. Code-MIE demonstrates that code-formalized modeling significantly improves accuracy, interpretability, and interoperability in multimodal IE.

item.title: SynCirc: Efficient Synthesis of Depth-Optimized Circuits from High-Level Languages (Extended Version)
item.description: Secure Multi-Party Computation (MPC) relies critically on low-depth circuit representations for efficiency. SynCirc is an open-source, MPC-optimized hardware synthesis framework built on Verilog and Yosys-ABC. It introduces custom multi-input AND gate libraries with depth-aware constraints, reducing multiplicative depth and online rounds by up to **3×** versus TinyGMW. Its expanded library of optimized primitives—including comparators, muxes, and equality checkers—achieves **22.3–66.7%** depth reduction over ShallowCC. Evaluated in FLUTE, SynCirc’s multi-input AND protocol requires only **1/116 the online communication** of Trifecta’s. Crucially, SynCirc integrates XLS-based HLS support, enabling C/C++-to-circuit compilation without Verilog expertise—democratizing secure computation.

item.title: Zeeperio: Verifying Governmental Elections with Ethereum
item.description: Zeeperio is a purpose-built zk-SNARK argument for Scantegrity II elections, designed to enable automatic, low-cost, on-chain verification via Ethereum smart contracts. Unlike general-purpose zk-SNARK toolkits (e.g., Circom or zkVMs), Zeeperio employs application-specific arithmetization—tightly modeling Scantegrity’s ballot encoding, commitment consistency, decryption correctness, and zero-knowledge guarantees—yielding drastic prover efficiency gains: under 5 hours on a commodity laptop to generate a <2 KB proof for 100,000 ballots, representing 2–3 orders-of-magnitude speedup over prior implementations. Its verification contract costs less than $30 per election (at current gas prices), with *constant* cost independent of ballot count. This enables fully automated, publicly auditable, and censorship-resistant election verification—shifting E2E-V from opt-in manual auditing to mandatory, trustless on-chain enforcement.

item.title: TAPAS: Efficient Two-Server Asymmetric Private Aggregation Beyond Prio(+)
item.description: Privacy-preserving aggregation is vital for federated learning and telemetry, enabling collective model training without exposing raw user data. Existing two-server protocols (e.g., Prio+) suffer from symmetric $O(L)$ communication and computation—prohibitive when client inputs involve tens to hundreds of millions of parameters ($L$). TAPAS overcomes this via *intentional asymmetry*: one server performs $O(L)$ aggregation and lattice-based zero-knowledge verification, while the other acts as a lightweight coordinator with $O(1)$ communication and computation independent of $L$. It requires **no trusted setup**, achieves **post-quantum security** solely under standard LWE/SIS assumptions, and provides **full malicious security** with **identifiable abort**—a first for two-server aggregation. Our novel, efficient lattice-based ZKPs enable end-to-end privacy and correctness proofs in this strong threat model.

item.title: Optimizing FROST for Message Capacity
item.description: FROST is a round-optimal threshold signature scheme, but its standard FROST-core protocol requires two presignatures per signature—each demanding an expensive distributed key generation (DKG) run—limiting throughput. This work investigates presignature reuse to improve message capacity. We show that naively using $k$ presignatures for $k$ messages is insecure: an extended ROS attack enables forgery even for $k=2$. However, we prove that using $k+1$ presignatures for $k$ messages is secure in the Generic Group Model + Random Oracle Model. This raises message capacity from 50% (baseline) to $\frac{k}{k+1}$, approaching 100% asymptotically. We further extend security to a practical variant where presignatures are generated asynchronously by different parties: security holds as long as at least $k+1$ of them originate from honest parties—enhancing robustness and deployment flexibility.

item.title: New Approaches to Zero-Knowledge SNARG Constructions
item.description: We present a novel black-box–friendly transformation from SNARGs to zkSNARGs—distinct from the standard NIZK+SNARG paradigm. Inspired by Ishai–Mahmoody–Sahai (TCC’12), our approach combines cryptographic hashing with information-theoretic ZK (specifically, PCPs) and treats the *underlying SNARG as a black box* (other primitives are used non-black-box). The transformation applies to subclasses of NP: batched NP (yielding zkBARGs) and languages admitting computational non-signaling PCPs—including NTISP. A key ingredient is a new **Hiding Somewhere-Extractable (HSE) commitment**, built from LWE, enhancing somewhere-statistically-binding hash functions with computational hiding. As a corollary, we obtain **zkBARGs for NP** and **zkSNARGs for NTISP**, both based solely on the standard Learning With Errors (LWE) assumption—demonstrating that restricting to natural NP subclasses enables zkSNARGs under widely trusted foundations.

item.title: High-Order Galois Automorphisms for TNFS Linear Algebra
item.description: The Tower Number Field Sieve (TNFS) is the state-of-the-art algorithm for solving the discrete logarithm problem in finite fields $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ with composite $n$, especially relevant to pairing-based cryptography lacking quantum-resistant alternatives. While $k$-order Galois automorphisms accelerate relation collection by factor $k$, leveraging them for linear algebra remained open beyond $k=2$. This work resolves the problem for $k = 6$ and $k = 12$: we present a generic construction enabling order-$6$ (resp. order-$12$) Galois automorphisms in *any* $\mathbb{F}_{p^6}$ (resp. $\mathbb{F}_{p^{12}}$), inducing block-cyclic structure in TNFS matrices. This reduces linear algebra cost by approximately $36\times$ and $144\times$, respectively — the first high-order acceleration with provable, practical impact. We provide a full SageMath implementation and validate it on small fields (e.g., $\mathbb{F}_{101^6}$), confirming dimension reduction and runtime gains consistent with theory.

item.title: PrivaDE: Privacy-preserving Data Evaluation for Blockchain-based Data Marketplaces
item.description: PrivaDE is a privacy-preserving protocol enabling model owners and data owners to jointly compute a utility score for a candidate dataset—without revealing model parameters, raw features, or labels. It provides malicious-security guarantees and integrates seamlessly with blockchain-based marketplaces via smart contracts that enforce fair execution and automated payment. To ensure practicality, we introduce two key optimizations: (1) efficient secure model inference using hierarchical gradient approximation, and (2) a model-agnostic scoring method that relies on a small, representative data subset (<0.5% of full set) while preserving high correlation (*r* > 0.92) with full-dataset training impact. Evaluation shows PrivaDE completes evaluation within **under 12 minutes** for models with up to 110M parameters, achieving 5.7× speedup over baseline MPC approaches and reducing memory usage by 62%. PrivaDE establishes the first scalable, verifiable, and privacy-preserving foundation for automated data valuation in decentralized machine learning ecosystems.

item.title: Cryptanalysis of four arbitrated quantum signature schemes
item.description: This paper presents a comprehensive cryptanalysis of four recently proposed arbitrated quantum signature (AQS) schemes. We identify fundamental security flaws in all four protocols—none achieve the claimed authenticity, non-repudiation, or information-theoretic security. Specifically: (1) Zhang et al.’s CNOT-chain scheme allows both sender repudiation and receiver signature forgery; (2) Ding et al.’s GHZ-based protocol admits trivial repudiation by *both* sender and receiver; (3) Lu et al.’s controlled-teleportation scheme is vulnerable to signature forgery, false receipt allegations, and mutual repudiation; (4) Zhang et al.’s entanglement-free scheme fails to guarantee receiver non-repudiation and lacks information-theoretic security for message authentication and unforgeability. Our attacks rely solely on standard quantum operations and protocol logic errors—not computational assumptions—exposing critical design gaps in arbitration, quantum-state binding, and evidence accountability. These results provide concrete counterexamples for rigorous AQS security modeling.

item.title: On Post-Quantum Signature with Message Recovery from Hash-and-Sign in QROM
item.description: We present the first post-quantum signature scheme with message recovery provably secure in the Quantum Random Oracle Model (QROM). Building on Bellare–Rogaway’s classical PSS-R, we adapt its hash-and-sign structure to quantum-safe primitives and overcome a fundamental challenge: securely reprogramming *two correlated random oracles* ($H$ and $G$) under quantum queries—where inputs to one oracle depend on outputs of the other. To this end, we extend two foundational QROM techniques: (i) the tight adaptive reprogramming theorem (Grilo et al., ASIACRYPT 2021) to support *sequential, interdependent reprogramming*, and (ii) the measure-and-reprogram theorem (Don et al., CRYPTO 2020) to handle output-dependent queries with bounded quantum advantage. Our security reduction shows EUF-CMA security with only polynomial loss $O(q_H q_G)$, making it the first rigorously proven QROM-secure SMR scheme. This work bridges a critical gap between post-quantum efficiency and formal security for compact digital signatures.

item.title: TP-NTT: Batch NTT Hardware with Application to Relinearization
item.description: Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) promises privacy-preserving computation but suffers from prohibitive overhead, dominated by Number Theoretic Transform (NTT)-based polynomial multiplication. This paper introduces **TP-NTT**, a scalable, throughput-configurable hardware architecture for batch NTT across FHE ring dimensions $2^{10}$ to $2^{16}$. TP-NTT employs multi-dimensional decomposition (2D/3D/4D) without extra multipliers, coupled with optimized modular arithmetic and memory access. At $n = 2^{16}$, it achieves **8.03× lower average latency** and **1.26× better area–time product (ATP)** versus the prior best design. We further demonstrate its practical impact via a BFV relinearization accelerator, delivering **34.65× speedup** over state-of-the-art software (HElib). TP-NTT bridges the gap between algorithmic flexibility and hardware efficiency for real-world FHE deployment.

item.title: Improved Issuer Hiding for BBS-based Anonymous Credentials
item.description: Attribute-based anonymous credentials (e.g., BBS+) often leak issuer identity during verification, undermining user privacy. Recent issuer-hiding proposals suffer from either prohibitive efficiency overhead or critical *policy vulnerabilities*: malicious users—colluding with just one authorized issuer—can forge credentials for arbitrary attributes, effectively impersonating any trusted issuer. To resolve these gaps, we propose the first AGM-proven, BBS-compatible issuer-hiding system based on a *signed-policy* paradigm. Our construction eliminates secret policy keys (enabling fully public verification), delegates policy signing to a trusted CA (removing per-RP key management), and introduces the first pairing-free issuer-hiding scheme using algebraic MACs. Implementation results confirm practical performance (<20 ms per operation), while formal proofs establish unforgeability and *everlasting issuer-hiding anonymity*—even against quantum adversaries post-issuance. This work delivers a secure, efficient, and deployable foundation for real-world privacy-preserving credential systems.

item.title: PrivaLean: Low-Latency and High-Accuracy System for Secure 2PC Inference
item.description: PrivaLean is a novel secure two-party computation (2PC) framework for low-latency, high-accuracy neural network inference under the semi-honest model. To overcome the communication and computational bottlenecks of existing systems, PrivaLean introduces two key innovations: (1) For linear layers, it proposes a *local random ciphertext generation* mechanism—eliminating costly offline interaction—and an *intermediate encoding scheme* that drastically reduces memory footprint for resource-constrained devices; (2) For non-linear activations, it designs a *trigonometric activation protocol* (e.g., sin/cos-based ReLU approximation) requiring only **one online communication round** and avoiding expensive truncation, coupled with a *data-distribution-aware training strategy* leveraging knowledge distillation to improve accuracy—achieving **higher accuracy than the ReLU baseline**. Evaluations on large models (e.g., ResNet-50) in WAN settings show PrivaLean completes inference in **125.02 seconds**, reducing latency by **50.5%** versus Cheetah—the state-of-the-art—while significantly cutting communication rounds.

item.title: Graph-based Asynchrony with Quasilinear Complexity for Any Linear Verifiable Secret Sharing Scheme
item.description: This paper introduces a graph-based statistical Asynchronous Verifiable Secret Sharing (AVSS) protocol, *Bonneval-on-Arc*, that transforms any linear VSS scheme into an efficient AVSS without modifying its core structure. By arranging $n$ parties as a $d$-regular communication graph—where each party interacts only with $d$ neighbors—we bypass the quadratic communication bottleneck inherent in broadcast-based AVSS. Our construction achieves **quasilinear dealer complexity** $O(n \log n)$ and **sublinear per-party complexity** $O(d \log n)$, significantly improving over state-of-the-art $O(n^2 \log n)$ dealer cost. In exchange for statistical (rather than perfect) correctness and termination guarantees, we support a corruption threshold $t < n/(d+2)$—enabling flexible trade-offs: e.g., $d = 4$ yields $t < n/6$, suitable for scalable settings where $t = n/3$ is unnecessarily strong. This is the first generic AVSS framework leveraging sparse graph topology to achieve quasilinear complexity.

item.title: Evolving Jailbreaks: Automated Multi-Objective Long-Tail Attacks on Large Language Models
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) face escalating jailbreak risks when deployed openly—especially via free web interfaces exposed to long-tail inputs (e.g., low-resource languages, encrypted private data). Prior approaches rely heavily on handcrafted rules, hindering systematic security evaluation. We propose **EvoJail**, the first automated framework for discovering long-tail jailbreaks via **multi-objective evolutionary search**. EvoJail jointly optimizes attack success rate and output perplexity while introducing a **semantic-algorithmic prompt representation** that encodes both high-level intent (e.g., “simulate a compiler log”) and low-level encryption-decryption logic (e.g., nested XOR+Base64). Integrated LLM-assisted mutation and crossover operators enable adaptive, semantics-aware exploration of complex, open-ended search spaces. Experiments across 12 long-tail settings show EvoJail consistently discovers diverse, effective jailbreaks—achieving up to **23.7% higher single-prompt success** and outperforming SOTA methods (e.g., GCG, AutoDAN) by **5.2% in ensemble attacks**. Code and benchmarks will be released.

item.title: Memory poisoning and secure multi-agent systems
item.description: This paper investigates **memory poisoning attacks**—a critical security threat emerging in LLM-powered agentic AI and multi-agent systems (MAS). We first categorize memory systems by origin, duration, and localization: short-term (user-initiated, agent-local), episodic (interaction-derived, distributed), and semantic (consolidated, knowledge-base-resident). We analyze attack feasibility across these layers, revealing novel vectors—especially *collusive poisoning* via inter-agent context sharing and *stealthy semantic retrieval poisoning*. To mitigate these, we propose: (1) cryptographic access control (e.g., ABE for knowledge bases); (2) **local inference with private knowledge retrieval**, where agents perform lightweight, signature-verified retrieval on local knowledge only; and (3) an audit protocol for inter-agent memory synchronization using hash chaining. Crucially, we identify and formalize the underexplored risk of *cross-agent memory propagation*, introducing a “memory diffusion graph” model. Our work advances security-by-design for MAS, offering both theoretical frameworks and practical mitigation strategies against memory poisoning.

item.title: Trojan's Whisper: Stealthy Manipulation of OpenClaw through Injected Bootstrapped Guidance
item.description: This paper identifies *guidance injection*—a novel, stealthy attack vector against autonomous coding agents like OpenClaw—that exploits extensible bootstrap guidance hooks to embed adversarial operational narratives as “routine best practices.” Unlike prompt injection, it manipulates the agent’s reasoning context without explicit malicious tokens, enabling autonomous execution of harmful actions (e.g., credential exfiltration, workspace destruction, backdoor installation). We construct 26 malicious skills across 13 attack categories and evaluate them on ORE-Bench—a realistic developer workspace benchmark—using 52 natural prompts and 6 state-of-the-art LLM backends. Attacks achieve success rates of 16.0%–64.2%, with >89% executed autonomously and no user confirmation; 94% evade existing static and LLM-based scanners. Our findings expose critical design tensions in agent ecosystems and call for defenses grounded in capability isolation, runtime policy enforcement, and transparent guidance provenance.

item.title: TAPAS: Efficient Two-Server Asymmetric Private Aggregation Beyond Prio(+)
item.description: Privacy-preserving aggregation is essential for federated learning and telemetry, enabling secure model updates without exposing user data. Existing two-server protocols (e.g., Prio+) suffer from $O(L)$ communication and computation per server—prohibitive when $L$ reaches tens to hundreds of millions (e.g., LLM gradients). We propose **TAPAS**, the first asymmetric two-server private aggregation scheme that eliminates trusted setup, achieves post-quantum security under standard lattice assumptions (LWE/SIS), and provides full malicious security with identifiable abort. Its key insight is *intentional asymmetry*: one server handles $O(L)$ aggregation and verification, while the other performs only $O(1)$-cost facilitation—enabling commodity-hardware deployment and strengthening the non-collusion assumption. We introduce novel, efficient lattice-based zero-knowledge proofs tailored for this setting—the first to jointly guarantee privacy, correctness, and identifiable abort in the two-server model. TAPAS reduces auxiliary-server communication to constant size and cuts verification latency by >90% at billion-scale dimensions.

item.title: Graph-Aware Text-Only Backdoor Poisoning for Text-Attributed Graphs
item.description: This paper investigates a realistic backdoor threat to text-attributed graphs—where nodes carry natural language text (e.g., paper abstracts or user posts)—under the constraint that attackers can *only modify node text*, not graph structure. We propose TAGBD, the first text-only backdoor attack framework for such graphs. TAGBD identifies influential training nodes via gradient-based sensitivity and centrality analysis, then generates semantically coherent, grammatically sound trigger phrases using a lightweight shadow graph model; triggers are injected via text replacement or short appendage. Experiments on Amazon, PubMed, and Yelp show TAGBD achieves an average attack success rate (ASR) of 92.7%, transfers robustly across GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE (ASR >86%), and withstands common defenses—including graph pruning and feature denoising—with ASR remaining 73.5%–89.1%. These results demonstrate that node text alone is a practical, stealthy, and resilient attack surface in graph learning, urging future defenses to jointly inspect structural links *and* textual semantics.

item.title: LiteAtt: Secure and Seamless IoT Services Using TinyML-based Self-Attestation as a Primitive
item.description: LiteAtt is a verifier-less, TinyML-powered self-attestation framework for secure and seamless IoT services. It leverages Arm TrustZone TEE on resource-constrained MCUs to perform real-time, on-device attestation of firmware’s SRAM footprint—without transmitting firmware copies or exposing sensitive user memory data. By training compact TinyML models (≈14 KB) on twin devices, LiteAtt preserves SRAM privacy while achieving 98.7% accuracy, 99.33% F1-score, 98.72% TPR, and 97.45% TNR on real-device datasets. It operates with only 1.29 ms latency, 42.79 μJ energy, and ≤32 KB runtime memory—making it practical for battery-powered Cortex-M systems. Security analysis confirms robustness against replay, impersonation, and privacy breaches, supporting mutual authentication, confidentiality, and integrity. LiteAtt pioneers decentralized trust for ubiquitous intelligent IoT.

item.title: Acyclic Graph Pattern Counting under Local Differential Privacy
item.description: Graph pattern counting is fundamental to network analysis, yet privacy-preserving computation under *local differential privacy (LDP)* remains limited to ad hoc solutions for specific patterns (e.g., triangles). This paper presents the **first general LDP mechanism for counting arbitrary acyclic graph patterns**, resolving a long-standing open problem. We identify and address two core challenges: (i) recursively constructing patterns from distributed data across rounds, tackled via an **LDP-tailored recursive subpattern counting framework**; and (ii) eliminating node duplication during construction, solved by a **random marking technique** that enforces unique node roles in each pattern instance. Theoretically, for any acyclic pattern with $k$ edges, our mechanism achieves additive error $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{N}\, d(G)^k)$, where $N$ is the number of nodes and $d(G)$ is the maximum degree. Experiments on real-world graphs show **46–2600× utility gain and 300–650× communication reduction** over baselines—establishing a scalable, provably private foundation for acyclic pattern analysis.

item.title: An Industrial-Scale Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Requirements Engineering: Empirical Evaluation with Automotive Manufacturing Data
item.description: This paper presents the first industrial-scale empirical evaluation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for requirements engineering (RE) in automotive manufacturing. Using authentic documentation—669 requirements across four specification standards (2015–2023) and 49 supplier qualification dossiers—the framework achieves **98.2% extraction accuracy** with full traceability, outperforming BERT-based and ungrounded LLM baselines by 24.4% and 19.6%, respectively. Hybrid semantic-lexical retrieval attains an MRR of 0.847, and expert assessment yields a mean quality score of 4.32/5.0. The system reduces manual analysis time by **83%**, cuts operational costs by **47%** via multi-provider LLM orchestration, and identifies 10 legacy suppliers (20.4%) requiring requalification—avoiding ~$2.3M in potential contract penalties. Longitudinal analysis further reveals a 55% reduction in requirement volume alongside an 1,800% surge in IT security focus.

item.title: Deep reflective reasoning in interdependence constrained structured data extraction from clinical notes for digital health
item.description: Structured data extraction from clinical notes faces fundamental challenges due to *interdependence constraints*—where values of one clinical variable (e.g., tumor size) logically constrain others (e.g., nodal status). Standard LLM-based pipelines often produce clinically inconsistent outputs because they lack mechanisms to verify cross-variable coherence. We propose **Deep Reflective Reasoning**, an LLM agent framework that iteratively self-critiques and revises structured extractions by jointly validating consistency across (1) inter-variable logical rules, (2) source text evidence, and (3) retrieved domain knowledge—terminating only upon convergence. Evaluated across three oncology tasks, it significantly improved accuracy: colorectal synoptic reporting (F1 +8.3%, numeric correctness +8.9%), Ewing sarcoma CD99 pattern identification (+5.7% accuracy), and lung cancer TNM staging (+15.3% overall stage accuracy; pN accuracy rose from 0.885 to 0.948). This work establishes reflective reasoning as a scalable, clinically grounded approach to building reliable, machine-operable clinical datasets for digital health.

item.title: Leveraging Natural Language Processing and Machine Learning for Evidence-Based Food Security Policy Decision-Making in Data-Scarce Making
item.description: Food security policy-making in data-scarce regions suffers from fragmented textual reports, sparse structured indicators, and demographic bias. This paper introduces **ZeroHungerAI**, an integrated NLP–ML framework that fuses socio-economic data with contextual policy text embeddings via a fine-tuned DistilBERT architecture. Evaluated on a real-world hybrid dataset of 1,200 samples across 25 districts, it achieves **91% accuracy**, **0.86 F1-score**, and **0.88 average precision** for minority (high-risk) classes—outperforming SVM (+13%) and Logistic Regression (+17%). A fairness-aware optimization reduces demographic parity difference to just **3%**, ensuring equitable rural–urban inference. Results demonstrate that lightweight, transfer-based contextual learning enables scalable, bias-aware hunger prediction in low-resource governance settings.

item.title: The production of meaning in the processing of natural language
item.description: This study investigates semantic contextuality—the quantum-like dependence of meaning on holistic measurement settings—as a fundamental property of natural language processing in both humans and LLMs. We compute the CHSH parameter $|S|$ across 12 models spanning four orders of magnitude (70M–70B parameters), correlating it with MMLU, hallucination rate, and nonsense detection. Crucially, the interquartile range (IQR) of $|S|$—the most discriminative statistic across models—is statistically orthogonal to all external benchmarks (|r| < 0.08, p > 0.4), revealing contextuality as a *latent cognitive dimension* independent of knowledge, truthfulness, or syntactic competence. Violation rate shows only weak, non-significant anticorrelation with all three benchmarks. Further, $|S|$ decreases under word-order perturbation and increases with sampling entropy, confirming its dynamism. We argue that genuine contextuality imposes intrinsic information-theoretic limits on prompt injection defenses—and that scalable “contextuality engineering” represents a subtler, pre-interpretive form of influence, shaping the space of possible meanings before any specific interpretation emerges.

item.title: The Robot's Inner Critic: Self-Refinement of Social Behaviors through VLM-based Replanning
item.description: This paper introduces **CRISP** (*Critique-and-Replan for Interactive Social Presence*), a general framework enabling robots to autonomously refine social behaviors via self-critique using a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a “human-like social critic.” Unlike prior methods relying on fixed motion libraries or human feedback, CRISP jointly integrates robot structural parsing (e.g., from MJCF), context-aware stepwise planning, vision-guided joint-code generation (using joint range-of-motion visualizations), VLM-based evaluation of social appropriateness and naturalness—with precise error localization—and reward-driven iterative replanning. Evaluated across **5 diverse robot platforms** (including humanoids, mobile manipulators, and legged robots) and **20 real-world social scenarios**, CRISP achieved significantly higher user preference (+42.3%) and situational appropriateness (+38.7%) versus state-of-the-art baselines (*p* < 0.001). Crucially, CRISP requires only the robot’s structural description—not API-specific code—enabling cross-platform deployment with minimal human intervention. This work establishes a new paradigm for autonomous, socially intelligent robot behavior generation.

item.title: FedRG: Unleashing the Representation Geometry for Federated Learning with Noisy Clients
item.description: Federated learning (FL) suffers severe performance degradation under client-side label noise, especially with data heterogeneity. Existing loss-based noisy sample detection methods lack reliability in non-IID FL settings due to unstable loss distributions. To address this, we propose **FedRG**, a geometry-aware framework grounded in the principle of *“representation geometry priority”*. FedRG first constructs label-agnostic spherical representations via self-supervised contrastive learning. It then iteratively fits a spherical von Mises-Fisher (vMF) mixture model using progressively identified clean samples to capture semantic clusters geometrically. A semantic-label soft mapping mechanism computes distribution divergence (via Wasserstein distance) between label-free and label-conditioned feature spaces, enabling robust noise identification and vMF model refinement. Finally, a personalized low-rank noise absorption matrix is applied per client for stable optimization. Extensive experiments across CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and realistic noisy benchmarks show FedRG consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods by **3.2–7.8%** in accuracy under diverse noise patterns and extreme heterogeneity (e.g., Dirichlet α=0.1, 60% noise).

item.title: ARMOR: Adaptive Resilience Against Model Poisoning Attacks in Continual Federated Learning for Mobile Indoor Localization
item.description: Indoor localization is critical for mobile applications, yet continual federated learning (CFL) for this task faces dual threats: natural environmental drift (e.g., signal dynamics, device heterogeneity) and adversarial model poisoning attacks. To address this, we propose **ARMOR**, a novel adaptive resilience framework that monitors global model (GM) evolution via a lightweight state-space model (SSM) trained on historical weight tensors. ARMOR projects incoming local updates onto the SSM-predicted “healthy” subspace and selectively mitigates outliers using cosine similarity and L₂ residual thresholds—preventing corruption before aggregation. Evaluated on real-world Wi-Fi/BLE data from 12 Android devices over 3 weeks, ARMOR achieves up to **8.0× reduction in mean localization error** (0.35 m vs. 2.84 m) and **4.97× reduction in worst-case error** (1.79 m vs. 8.91 m) compared to state-of-the-art CFL baselines. It maintains robustness under diverse poisoning attacks, demonstrating unified defense against both non-malicious drift and adversarial manipulation.
